coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot impregnated g-C3N4/SnS2 nanocomposite as an efficient mediator and co-catalyst for enhanced photocatalytic degradation and water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Saadati, Amir
Sheibani, Saeed
coredata.dc:description:
Noble metal electron mediators and co-catalysts have prevented further development of indirect Z-scheme systems for photocatalytic applications. Carbonaceous candidates have been one of the best alternatives to address this issue. For this purpose, in the present study, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-DCD) impregnated g-C3N4/SnS2 heterostructure has been successfully synthesized with optimized contents of N-DCDs and SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes through thermal-impregnation and reflux methods. The most efficient nanocomposite containing 0.25 wt% N-DCDs and 2 wt% SnS2 exhibited methylene blue (MB) degradation rate constant of 0.032 min−1 and hydrogen (H2) production efficiency of 1622.2 μmol/g.h after 120 and 180 min visible light irradiation. The photodegradation rate constant was almost 32, 8, and 2, and H2 production efficiency was 16, 30, and 5 fold higher than g-C3N4, SnS2, and g-C3N4/2 wt% SnS2 (without N-DCDs), respectively. The photodegradation and H2 production efficiencies are enhanced by impregnated N-DCDs, which act as an electron mediator and noble metal-free co-catalyst. Through N-DCDs impregnation, the synergistic effect between SnS2 and up-conversion properties of N-DCDs was observed, resulting in the reduction of the band gap energy from 2.97 eV in g-C3N4 to 2.80 eV in the optimized ternary nanocomposite, as well as reductions in PL intensity and onset potential. The major active species throughout photocatalytic degradation reactions were superoxide and hydroxyl radicals, proposing a Z-scheme mechanism for charge transfer. The optimized ternary nanocomposite also exhibited outstanding photostability after recycling tests. This work highlighted the role of N-DCDs as a highly effective noble metal-free co-catalyst and electron mediator in photocatalytic applications.
Data Availability Statement The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to legal or ethical reasons.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD of (a) g-C3N4, N-DCD, and N-DCD impregnated g-C3N4 with different content, and (b) SnS2 and different SnS2-containing nanocomposites.

FESEM images of (a) SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes with (b) the corresponding EDS and mapping images, (c) g-C3N4, (d) 0.25 C-C3N4, (e) 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposites with (f) the corresponding EDS and mapping images. (all the FESEM insets show high-magnification images).

(a) 2D AFM image with (b) the corresponding profile along lines A, B, C, and D. (c) 2D AFM image of N-DCD with the 3D reconstruction of the line E region, (d) 1D power spectral density of the AFM image and height profile along line E.

TEM images with the SAED patterns in the insets, HRTEM images, and the corresponding FFT and inverse FFT images of (a), (b), and (c) g-C3N4 nanosheets, (d), (e), and (f) g-C3N4/2SnS2, (g), (h), and (i) 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposites.

(a) UV–vis DRS and PL emission spectra of N-DCDs with photographs of N-DCDs solution. (UV light (right), and visible light (left)). (b) PL spectra at the different excitation wavelengths of the N-DCDs.

UV–vis DRS spectra with the Tauc plots of (a) and (b) g-C3N4 and N-DCDs impregnated g-C3N4, and (c) and (d) SnS2 and SnS2-containing nanocomposites (the insets show optical photographs of different samples). (e) and (f) PL spectra of different samples.
(a) EIS plots of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 samples (b) with the corresponding equivalent circuit.
(a) and (c) Photodegradation of MB solution (2 mg/mL) over different samples under visible light irradiation, (b) and (d) the calculated k values of different photocatalysts.

(a) C/C0 vs. time plots and (b) the obtained efficiency toward photodegradation of MB solution (2 mg/mL) over 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite in the presence of various scavengers. Mott-Schottky curves of (c) g-C3N4, (b) SnS2, and (c) 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 samples.
Schematic representation of the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism for in the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite for photocatalytic reactions.

The results of the three-cycle recycling experiments: (a) photodegradation efficiency. (b) XRD patterns in the as-synthesized and used state, FESEM image with high magnification in the inset, and (d) EDS point spectra of the red arrow and the corresponding C, N, Sn, and S mapping images of the red-marked area of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite.

(a) H2 production, (b) the calculated kH2 values of SnS2, g-C3N4, g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 under visible light irradiation, (c) H2 production recycling experiment in the presence of 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite, and (d) LSV curves of the samples.
Photocatalytic H2 production efficiency in different g-C3N4-based nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot impregnated g-C3N4/SnS2 nanocomposite as an efficient mediator and co-catalyst for enhanced photocatalytic degradation and water splitting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Noble metal electron mediators and co-catalysts have prevented further development of indirect Z-scheme systems for photocatalytic applications. Carbonaceous candidates have been one of the best alternatives to address this issue. For this purpose, in the present study, a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dot (N-DCD) impregnated g-C3N4/SnS2 heterostructure has been successfully synthesized with optimized contents of N-DCDs and SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes through thermal-impregnation and reflux methods. The most efficient nanocomposite containing 0.25 wt% N-DCDs and 2 wt% SnS2 exhibited methylene blue (MB) degradation rate constant of 0.032 min−1 and hydrogen (H2) production efficiency of 1622.2 μmol/g.h after 120 and 180 min visible light irradiation. The photodegradation rate constant was almost 32, 8, and 2, and H2 production efficiency was 16, 30, and 5 fold higher than g-C3N4, SnS2, and g-C3N4/2 wt% SnS2 (without N-DCDs), respectively. The photodegradation and H2 production efficiencies are enhanced by impregnated N-DCDs, which act as an electron mediator and noble metal-free co-catalyst. Through N-DCDs impregnation, the synergistic effect between SnS2 and up-conversion properties of N-DCDs was observed, resulting in the reduction of the band gap energy from 2.97 eV in g-C3N4 to 2.80 eV in the optimized ternary nanocomposite, as well as reductions in PL intensity and onset potential. The major active species throughout photocatalytic degradation reactions were superoxide and hydroxyl radicals, proposing a Z-scheme mechanism for charge transfer. The optimized ternary nanocomposite also exhibited outstanding photostability after recycling tests. This work highlighted the role of N-DCDs as a highly effective noble metal-free co-catalyst and electron mediator in photocatalytic applications.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to legal or ethical reasons.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial development and urban expansion are increasing environmental challenges and decreasing the most widely used energy resources, i.e., fossil fuels, in the world. Solar power utilization for triggering photochemical reactions has been highlighted as a limitless power resource to overcome energy and environmental crises. Eco-friendliness, cost-efficiency, and universality are significant advantages of using sunlight. So, wastewater treatment and H2 production by employing a suitable semiconductor photocatalyst could meet the environmental requirements to develop green technologies for the future [1–3]. In order to further develop this field, various semiconductors have been employed to date. Different types of metal oxides, sulfides, and metal-organic frameworks have been investigated as photocatalysts [4–8].

Since 2009, when Wang et al. [9] employed graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as a visible-light-responsive photocatalyst for producing H2 from water, numerous reports have explored various aspects of this polymeric semiconductor [10–15]. Bulk g-C3N4 has the highest stability among different allotropes of carbon nitride, which possesses layered structure, thermal and chemical stability, and an uncomplicated polymerization process [16,17]. However, high recombination rate of e- (e-h) pairs in 2p hybrid orbitals of C and N atoms and low specific surface area have restricted further application of this polymeric photocatalyst [18,19]. Several methods for bulk g-C3N4 have been developed to decrease the recombination rate of e-h pairs and increase the specific surface area by reducing the number of layers in the graphitic structure [20]. Nevertheless, the quantum size effect leads to larger band gap energy of the g-C3N4 nanosheets hindering the photocatalytic performance under visible light radiation [21,22]. It has been reported that the extension in the visible-light harvesting of the nanosheets could be achieved by tin disulfide (SnS2) introduction [23]. Moreover, the suitable band structures of SnS2 and g-C3N4 nanosheets could separate the e-h pairs through a Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism. Over the past few years, the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the direct Z-scheme g-C3N4/SnS2 has been studied in depth [24–26]. However, it is well known that the presence of an electron mediator could facilitate the flow of the charge carriers [27]. Yin et al. [28] reported that Ag introduction in the g-C3N4/SnS2 composite led to a significant improvement in photocatalytic performance owing to the surface plasmon resonance and efficient e-h transfer in Ag-g-C3N4/SnS2. The same behavior was observed employing Au nanoparticles as an electron mediator [29]. But, high-priced noble metal introduction restricts widespread use of the indirect Z-scheme systems. Lately, carbonaceous nanomaterials, like graphene, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, and carbon dots, have attracted significant attention, which could pave the way for further development in the carbonaceous all-solid-state Z-scheme.

Carbon dots, as zero-dimensional nanomaterials, have been highlighted in the photocatalysis field owing to their unique fluorescence and photoelectron transfer properties [30,31]. Additionally, nitrogen doping in the carbon dot structure could improve the electron-accepting and donating behavior of the carbon dots [32]. To the best of our knowledge, N-DCD introduction in g-C3N4/SnS2 system has not been reported. In this study, the photocatalytic degradation and water splitting for H2 production by a novel ternary g-C3N4/N-DCD/SnS2 nanocomposite has been investigated, which has not been studied until now. N-DCDs, as electron mediators and noble metal-free co-catalyst, were first impregnated on the surface of the g-C3N4 nanosheets using a simple thermal-impregnation approach. The impregnation content of the N-DCDs was optimized. Then, SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes were grown on the optimized N-DCD impregnated g-C3N4 via a convenient reflux process. Then, the content of the in-situ grown SnS2 was also optimized. Eventually, the role of the impregnated N-DCDs in the nanocomposite during photocatalytic degradation and water splitting was clarified.

All the precursors were analytical grades (99.99% Sigma-Aldrich Co.) and used without purification. Melamine (C3H6N6), Citric acid (C6H8O7), Urea (CH4N2O), Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), Thioacetamide (C2H5NS), and Tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate (SnCl40.5 H2O) were used for the synthesis of samples. During the synthesis process, deionized water and absolute ethanol were employed to wash the samples. The g-C3N4 nanosheet synthesis was as follows: melamine and ammonium chloride with a mass ratio of 1:5 were mixed dryly. After placing the resulting mixture in an alumina crucible with a lid, thermal polymerization was conducted in a muffle furnace with a 10 °C/min ramp rate at 550 °C for 4 h. N-DCDs were synthesized through a facile microwave-assisted technique. Citric acid and urea with a mass ratio of 1:1 were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The resultant transparent solution was put in a microwave (900 W, 80% efficiency) for 5 min. The dark brown solid was dissolved in 250 mL of deionized water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove the larger particles. The N-DCDs powder was obtained via freeze-drying the dark brown solution. A facile reflux method was employed to synthesize SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes. A three-neck flask in an oil bath equipped with a thermometer, condenser, and an injection system. Firstly, 30 mL ethylene glycol was used to dissolve SnCl40.5 H2O and transferred to the three-neck flask. After that, a specific amount of C2H5NS, the molar ratio of C2H5NS: SnCl40.5 H2O was 4, dissolved in 30 mL ethylene glycol, and transferred to the as-mentioned injection system in the reflux apparatus. The prepared SnCl40.5 H2O solution in the reflux apparatus was heated. Once the temperature reached 160 °C, the injection system injected the C2H5NS solution into the three-neck flask and stirring continued for 1 h. Next, the three-neck flask was removed from the reflux apparatus to cool down. The obtained suspension was centrifuged to obtain the precipitate and washed with absolute ethanol and distilled water to remove impurities. By drying in an oven with a temperature of 60 °C for 12 h, SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes were achieved.

The preparation of N-DCD impregnated g-C3N4 through the thermal-impregnation method was as follows: first, N-DCD solution with a 1 g/L concentration was prepared. In a typical synthesis procedure, 100 mg g-C3N4 nanosheets were dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water. Then, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 mL of the prepared N-DCD solution were injected into the g-C3N4 suspension and stirred magnetically at room temperature for 12 h to impregnate N-DCD on the surface of the nanosheets. After drying the suspension at 100 °C overnight, the resultant powder calcinated at 300 °C for 1 h. The obtained N-DCD impregnated g-C3N4 with 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 wt% were denoted 0.1 C-C3N4, 0.25 C-C3N4, 0.5 C-C3N4, and 1 C-C3N4, respectively.

SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes were grown on 0.25 C-C3N4 and g-C3N4 nanosheets according to the following procedure: A calculated amount of 0.25 C-C3N4 nanosheets was dispersed in 30 mL ethylene glycol with a calculated amount of SnCl40.5 H2O and sonicated for 15 min. Then, the same procedure was followed for the pure SnS2. The calculated contents of SnS2 in the nanocomposite were 1, 2, 4, and 8 wt%, denoted as 0.25 C-C3N4/1SnS2, 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2, 0.25 C-C3N4/4SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/8SnS2, respectively. Moreover, 2 wt% SnS2 was grown on g-C3N4 nanosheets without N-DCD (denoted as g-C3N4/2SnS2) to clear up the role of the N-DCDs as electron mediators and co-catalyst.

A Phillips-PW3040/60 X-ray diffractometer was employed to investigate the crystallographic properties of the synthesized samples (Cu Kα, λ = 1.54 Å). The morphology of the as-prepared photocatalysts was studied by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM-CamScanMV2300 with EDS, JEOL, Centurio spectrometer) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM-FEI Tecnai G2-F20, 200 kV). TEM and HRTEM images of the samples were analyzed using Gatan Digital micrograph software. The topography of the g-C3N4 nanosheets and N-DCDs was evaluated employing a contact mode Veeco-TP2 atomic force microscope (AFM), and the obtained images were analyzed by Gwyddion software. The optical properties of the photocatalysts were evaluated employing diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS-Shimadzu MPC-2200) and PL (Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer, λex = 355 nm). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a conventional three-electrode cell. Working, counter, and reference electrodes were 2 mm diameter photocatalysts-coated glassy carbon, 2 mm diameter Platinum disk, and Calomel electrodes. The electrolyte was 10 mL Na2SO4 with a concentration of 0.2 M. The working electrode was prepared through the following procedure: dispersion of 3 mg photocatalysts in 100 μL ethanol through sonication for 10 min 3 μL of the suspension was dripped on the surface of the glassy carbon and dried under IR lamp radiation. At last, Nafion solution with a volume of 1 μL was added and dried as before. EIS curves were obtained at applied open circuit potential and a frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 1 MHz with a 0.1 V amplitude.The Mott-Schottky curve of the samples was plotted under dark conditions employing the three-electrode system (frequency = 500 Hz, amplitude = 10 mV). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was also conducted with the same electrode in the same condition (scan rate = 10 mV/S).

The photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts was investigated using MB as a pollutant with a dosage of 0.067 g/L (10 mg photocatalyst in 150 mL MB solution). An Orsam visible light source (150 W) with an ultraviolet (UV) cutoff filter was utilized to conduct the photocatalytic tests. The suspension of the photocatalyst and MB solution was subjected to visible light after 60 min dark to reach equilibrium in adsorption-desorption. At 30 min intervals until 180 min, the sampling (2 mL) and centrifuging were conducted to analyze the MB concentration using a UNICO 2100 UV–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (λ = 664 nm). Moreover, a blank test was performed through which MB solution without photocatalysts was subjected to visible light irradiation. The same photocatalytic experiments were conducted by adding radical scavengers to elucidate charge transfer in the optimized nanocomposite. The radical scavengers were Isopropanol (IPA, • OH), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA, h+), and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ, • O2 -) with concentrations of 3, 3, and 0.1 mM, respectively. A three-cycle recycling test was carried out to evaluate the photostability of the photocatalyst. After each cycle, the powder was washed and then dried.

Using a laboratory setup without Pt noble metal co-catalyst, the photocatalytic water splitting performance of the samples was assessed toward the production of H2. Fig. S1 demonstrates the schematic of the water-splitting apparatus. In this system, a well-gas-sealed quartz tube reactor was stirred under constant rotation and placed in the optical chamber. The visible-light chamber consisted of 9 yellow LED lamps, which applied irradiation in the center of the reactor. The system was connected to a cooling fan to prevent the temperature from rising. The inlet of the reactor was connected to an analog pressure gauge using a steel line, and the accumulated gas was injected into the inlet of a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). The evolved H2 was quantified employing gas chromatography with nitrogen as a carrier gas (GC-7900, 5 A molecular sieve) every 30 min, assuming linear response of the GC with the concentration of the evolved H2 within the concentration range of the photocatalytic H2 evolution and no H2 dissolution in the suspension. The detailed experimental conditions were as follows: 30 mg photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL sodium sulfite 0.1 M (sacrificial electron donor) with pH = 6.7 and then transferred to the tube reactor. The system was allowed to stir thoroughly for 1 h in the dark, and the lamps were turned on. At 30 min intervals, the produced H2 was measured until 180 min visible light irradiation was completed. Additionally, a four-cycle recycling experiment was conducted to evaluate H2 production efficiency after each cycle.

The obtained X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared samples are depicted in Fig. 1. The effect of the different impregnation contents on the g-C3N4 XRD pattern is displayed in Fig. 1(a). XRD pattern of the N-DCDs powder exhibited a broad low-intensity peak with a 2θ value of 27.5º which is attributed to the disordered carbon atoms responsible for (002) plane diffraction. This feature confirms the nanoscale and low crystalline nature of the N-DCDs sample. According to Bragg’s equation [33], the calculated d-spacing was approximately 0.321 nm. Comparing the inter-planar spacing with perfect graphite (d (002) = 0.335–0.340 nm) could prove nitrogen doping due to the smaller size of nitrogen than carbon. These results are consistent with literature reports [34–36]. XRD pattern of the g-C3N4 nanosheets showed two typical peaks at 12.9º, corresponding to the tri-s-triazine unit, and 27.8º, analogous to the stack of the layers, which could be indexed to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively (JCPDS No. 87–1526) [37]. Moreover, the broad and low-intense peak of the g-C3N4 nanosheets confirms the exfoliation process, i.e., reducing the number of layers, using ammonium chloride as a blowing agent [38]. Neither peak shift nor peak intensity reduction was observed by impregnating 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 wt% N-DCD, suggesting a well-preserved structure of the nanosheets during the thermal-impregnation method. Fig. 1(b) depicts the effect of the in-situ growth of SnS2 on the XRD pattern of 0.25 C-C3N4 and g-C3N4. As illustrated, the characteristic peaks of SnS2 nanoflakes showed hexagonal crystal structure, according to JCPDS No. 23–0677. Crystal planes with miller indices of (001), (100), (101), (110), (111), and (201) reflected at 14.98, 28.48, 32.74, 50.00, 52.54, and 60.82º, respectively. Additionally, the observed peak broadening could verify the successful synthesis of nanostructured SnS2 [39,40]. In-situ growth of 1, 2, and 4 wt% SnS2 on 0.25 C-C3N4 and 2 wt% SnS2 on g-C3N4 did not reflect the characteristic peaks of SnS2. However, once SnS2 content reached 8 wt%, (101), (110), and (111) crystal planes were reflected as labeled in Fig. 1(b). The enlarged pattern of 0.25 C-C3N4/4SnS2 and 0.25 C-C3N4/8SnS2 are compared in Fig. S2 (a, b) for further understanding. The mentioned peaks are evident as SnS2 content increased from 4 to 8 wt%.

Morphological aspects of the samples were evaluated and the FESEM images are shown in Fig. 2. The FESEM image of the ultra-thin SnS2 nanoflakes in Fig. 2(a) proves the successful synthesis of nanostructured SnS2 with relatively uniform diameters. The high magnification FESEM image has been brought in the inset of Fig. 2(a) to gain detailed information about the synthesized nanoflakes. As shown in the inset, the average diameter of these nanoflakes is approximately 35 nm. Fig. 2(b) shows the EDS spectrum and elemental (Sn, S) mapping images of the ultra-thin SnS2 nanoflakes. The high-intense peaks of Sn and S in the EDS spectrum without any impurities suggest the high purity of the synthesized nanoflakes. Elemental mapping (inset of Fig. 2(b)) in the red-marked area indicated that Sn and S atoms were uniformly distributed within the ultra-thin nanoflakes. Moreover, the results of the quantitative analysis (in the inset Table) were in agreement with Sn:S ratio in stoichiometric SnS2. Fig. 2(c) exhibits the FESEM image after thermal polymerization. As illustrated, the 2D form of the g-C3N4 nanosheets with micron-sized lateral dimension and nano-sized thickness is apparent. The low magnification image proves the uniform micron-sized dimension in nanosheets, and the inset in Fig. 2(a) shows the uniform nano-sized thickness of the polymerized nanosheets. According to previous reports [41,42], melamine polymerization leads to bulk g-C3N4 formation. But, NH4Cl decomposition during melamine polymerization could significantly decrease the degree of polymerization and enhance the exfoliation. As evidenced in Fig. 2(d), the 0.25 C-C3N4 sample illustrated the same morphological features as in g-C3N4 nanosheets, implying that the nanosheets preserved their structure during the thermal-impregnation procedure as already verified through the XRD patterns. Besides, N-DCDs were not detected in the FESEM images, mainly due to the ultrafine size of less than 10 nm. FESEM images with the corresponding EDS spectrum and C, N, Sn, and S mapping of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 2(e) and (f). Successful SnS2 nanoflakes in-situ growth on the g-C3N4 nanosheets with suitable contact is conspicuous from the inset of Fig. 2(e). To investigate more, mapping images of the red-marked area along with the obtained EDS spectrum (red arrow) corroborated the presence of Sn and S atoms with relatively uniform distribution on the g-C3N4 nanosheets. Furthermore, the quantitative analysis gave evidence that the atomic percentage of the elements in the sample is close enough to the theoretical values for synthesis.

AFM was employed to analyze the nanoscale surface topography of the synthesized g-C3N4 and N-DCDs. The obtained data for g-C3N4 nanosheets and N-DCDs are illustrated in Fig. 3(a) and (c), respectively. Based on the margin scale ruler of Fig. 3(a), the lateral size of the nanosheets varies from approximately 1 to 2 µm. The corresponding height profile along lines A, B, C, and D are shown in Fig. 3(b) which exhibited a thickness of 9.1, 4.8, 2.9, and 2.6 nm, respectively. Moreover, the serrations in the line A height profile might be attributed to the stack of several g-C3N4 nanosheets with less than 3 nm thickness. So, these results confirm the successful low degree of polymerization, in accordance with the XRD and FESEM results of the g-C3N4 nanosheets. Fig. 3(c) shows the AFM image of the as-synthesized N-DCDs. According to the 2D AFM image and 3D reconstruction of the area where line E is drawn, shown in the inset, spherical carbon dots with less than 10 nm are observable. Furthermore, 1D statistical calculation and height profile along line E are exhibited in Fig. 3(d). Statistical results from the obtained AFM image verified an average size of 5.9 nm, and the height profile along line E showed carbon dots with a height of 4.1, 2.6, and 4.4 nm.

For better comprehension, TEM and HRTEM images of the g-C3N4 nanosheets, g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 4. For g-C3N4 nanosheets, the TEM image (Fig. 4(a)) exhibited a relatively transparent nanosheet attributed to the successful exfoliation by introducing ammonium chloride. However, the amorphous nature of the nanosheets could be concluded from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 4(a) inset). The obtained ring pattern is the characteristic layer stacking distance of the nanosheets, related to the (002) planes according to the XRD pattern of g-C3N4 nanosheets [43]. As shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), the HRTEM image and the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (inset of Fig. 4(c)) with inverse FFT image were further proved in-plane amorphous structure of the g-C3N4 nanosheets, consistent with the SAED pattern. Fig. 4(d) represents the TEM image of the g-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite with the SAED pattern in the inset. The observed contrast on the g-C3N4 surfaces might result from the in-situ growth of the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes, which could be justified through the SAED pattern and the HRTEM image. Comparing the SAED pattern of bare g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite led to the conclusion that there were some faded bright spots on the outer surface of the full ring pattern of nanosheets containing a d-spacing of approximately 0.280 nm which could be related to the (101) crystal planes of SnS2. HRTEM image of the g-C3N4/2SnS2 in Fig. 4(e), along with the SnS2 (I) and g-C3N4 (II) region FFT pattern (Fig. 4(f)), confirmed the existence of the (101) planes of SnS2 on the g-C3N4 surface. Additionally, inverse FFT images of regions I and II could easily visualize the successful growth of SnS2 with a lattice fringe of about 0.275 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal planes on the amorphous g-C3N4 nanosheets. TEM images with the corresponding SAED pattern of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite are exhibited in Fig. 4(g). The inset SAED pattern revealed the presence of poor crystalline N-DCDs in the nanocomposite, which had an overlapped diffraction ring with g-C3N4 due to almost the same (002) peak in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1(a)). For more detailed information about the structure of the impregnated N-DCDs and in-situ grown SnS2 nanoflakes, the HRTEM image of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 sample was brought in Fig. 4(h). The observable lattice fringes in regions I and II could be attributed to the SnS2 and N-DCD. As shown in Fig. 4(i), the FFT pattern of region I exhibited the diffraction spots corresponding to the (101) crystal planes. According to the inverse FFT image in the right inset, the clear lattice fringes of about 0.278 nm further substantiated the in-situ growth of SnS2 nanoflakes. FFT with consequent inverse FFT was applied in region II to further understand and visualize the structure of the impregnated N-DCDs. The FFT pattern of the N-DCD displayed the (002) crystal plane spots, which as clarified in the inverse FFT image, sp2 hybridized carbon domains with calculated d-spacing of 0.320 nm formed the crystalline core of the N-DCDs. Moreover, the ID/IG ratio of approximately 0.7 from Raman spectra supported the idea of the crystalline core in N-DCDs, which is less compared to the complete amorphous carbon dots (Fig. S3) [44]. So, based on the FESEM and TEM images of the nanocomposites, the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes and N-DCDs have been successfully anchored on the surface of the g-C3N4 nanosheets, favorable for the charge transfer during photocatalytic reactions.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and PL were employed to evaluate the optical properties of the synthesized samples. The obtained results for N-DCDs are illustrated in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the absorption spectrum of the N-DCDs sample consists of three main absorbance peaks, centered at approximately 260, 334, and 404 nm. The first absorbance peak in the UV region could be attributed to the transition from the bonding π orbital to the antibonding π* orbital in CC bonds. The second arises from the transition of electrons from nonbonding electron pairs to an antibonding π* orbital in an oxygen or nitrogen-containing structure in the carbon dot [45]. The absorbance peak at 404 nm could result from the amino group on the N-DCDs surface [42]. Moreover, the N-DCDs powder showed excellent water-solubility (inset of Fig. 5(a), left) and greenish-blue luminescence emission under UV irradiation (right inset of Fig. 5(a)). The PL emission at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm exhibited a peak centered at approximately 490 nm. Fig. 5(b) shows the PL spectra from an excitation wavelength of 500 nm to near-infrared (NIR) excitation (up to 800 nm). As illustrated by longer wavelength excitation, the up-converted emissions are centered between 500 and 600 nm, which might be attributed to the multiphoton active process of the N-DCDs sample [46]. This remarkable feature of the N-DCDs sample could enhance the light-utilization of the photocatalysts since nitrogen doping leads to absorbing more photons with lower energy [47].

Optical properties evaluation of g-C3N4, N-DCDs impregnated g-C3N4, SnS2, and SnS2-containing nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 6. The absorbance spectra of the bare g-C3N4 nanosheets and N-DCDs impregnated g-C3N4 nanosheets with various contents are depicted in Fig. 6(a). As depicted, the absorption edge of the g-C3N4 nanosheets was approximately 435 nm. Reducing the number of layers during thermal polymerization led to a reduction in the conjugation length, directly affected by the precursor amount [48,49]. Ou et al. [50] prepared the few-layer g-C3N4 nanosheets through the sonication method with an absorption edge of 435 nm. Hence, thermal polymerization employing ammonium chloride with a low precursor amount could yield high-quality g-C3N4 nanosheets, consistent with the AFM image of the g-C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 3(a)). Increasing the absorption edge of the g-C3N4 nanosheets and redshifting were the results of loading N-DCDs through the thermal-impregnation method. The absorption edges of 0.1 C-C3N4, 0.25 C-C3N4, 0.5 C-C3N4, and 1 C-C3N4 reached from 435 nm to approximately 451, 460, 470, and 480 nm, respectively. The observed color transformation of the N-DCD impregnated samples in the inset of Fig. 6(a) agrees with the considerable redshift in these samples. Tauc plot (Eq. (1)) was employed for band gap calculation of the samples [51]:
(1) (αhυ)2=A(hυ-Eg)
Where, Eg, ν, h, A, and α are the band gap energy, frequency of vibrations, Planck constant, constant, and absorption coefficient, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 6(b), the calculated Eg of the g-C3N4 nanosheets was 2.97 eV, and for the N-DCD impregnated samples were 2.91, 2.84, 2.83, and 2.75 eV as the N-DCD content increased from 0.1 to 0.25, 0.5, and 1 wt%, respectively. This redshift and subsequent band gap reduction are mainly ascribed to the photosensitization and up-conversion ability of the impregnated N-DCD on the g-C3N4 nanosheets [52,53]. Absorbance spectra and the corresponding Tuac plots of the SnS2 and SnS2 in-situ grown nanocomposites are exhibited in Fig. 6(c) and (d) to enlighten the role of the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes on the optical characteristics of the nanocomposites. SnS2 nanoflakes possessed a broad absorption spectrum in the visible light region with approximately 650 nm absorption edge, in agreement with the previous reports [54,55]. Such optical properties could improve the absorption capability of the nanocomposites as the weight percent of the SnS2 increased. This phenomenon was observed in Fig. 6(c) when 2 wt% SnS2 and different SnS2 contents grew on g-C3N4 and 0.25 C-C3N4. Moreover, the corresponding photographs of the powders in the inset of Fig. 6(c) further confirmed the in-situ growth of the brown SnS2 nanoflakes. Based on these circumstances and as shown in Fig. 6(d), the calculated Eg of SnS2, 0.25 C-C3N4/1SnS2, g-C3N4/2SnS2, 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2, 0.25 C-C3N4/4SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/8SnS2 were 2.20, 2.87, 2.84, 2.80, 2.77, and 2.55 eV, respectively. Therefore, N-DCD impregnation along with in-situ growth of the ultra-thin SnS2 nanoflakes could significantly enhance the visible light-utilization of the photocatalysts.

PL spectra of the photocatalysts in 400–750 nm were collected to investigate the behavior of the e-h pair after excitation. It is a well-known fact that the more intense the PL peak, the more recombination of the e-h pairs occurs [56]. As depicted in Fig. 6(e), the PL intensity of the bare g-C3N4 remarkably declined once 0.1 wt% N-DCDs were impregnated on the surface of the nanosheets. Moreover, 0.25 C-C3N4 photocatalyst exhibited almost the same PL spectra as in 0.1 C-C3N4 photocatalyst, proposing efficient charge separation in presence of the impregnated N-DCDs. However, increasing N-DCD contents to 0.5 and 1 wt% led to appearing high-intense PL peaks compared to the 0.1 C-C3N4 and 0.25 C-C3N4 samples. Fang et al. [55] reported that as carbon dot content on the g-C3N4 nanosheets increases from specific content, PL intensity increases and surface photovoltage decreases, indicating that there is a critical content for carbon dot to reach the optimized properties. In other words, carbon dots can act as centers for the recombination of the e-h pairs when the content exceeds the critical content. In Fig. 6(f), the PL spectra of the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes, 0.25 C-C3N4, and various nanocomposites were compared to elucidate the role of the heterostructure formation on the recombination behavior of the samples. Pure SnS2 showed a high-intense PL peak consisting of two broad peaks centered approximately at 470 and 520 nm. The first one arises from sulfur defects in the lattice, and the latter could be ascribed to the bound exciton recombination with higher energy than the band gap energy of SnS2 [57,58]. 1 wt% in-situ growth of SnS2 on 0.25 C-C3N4 did not considerably affect the PL spectra. However, increasing the SnS2 weight percent in the nanocomposite to 2, 4, and 8 wt% resulted in a considerable PL intensity reduction, and 2 wt% was found to be the most efficient SnS2 weight percent. Moreover, comparing the g-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite PL spectra with 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 provided evidence that N-DCD impregnation could significantly hinder the recombination of the e-h pairs. So, based on the PL results, 0.25 wt% N-DCD and 2 wt% SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes were the optimized weight percent in the nanocomposite, suggesting that 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 could perform the best charge separation during photocatalytic reactions.

The EIS data of the g-C3N4, g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 samples are shown in Fig. 7(a) to illuminate the effect of the impregnated N-DCDs on the electron transfer across the electrode-electrolyte interface. These results were analyzed employing an equivalent circuit exhibited in Fig. 7(b), where Re is the ohmic resistance (electrode, electrolyte, and current collector resistance), CPE is the constant phase element, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, and ZW is the Warburg impedance. The arc radius in a Nyquist plot is inversely proportional to the effectiveness of the charge transfer [59,60]. The obtained Rct values for g-C3N4, g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 were 21.56, 15.09, and 3.86 kΩ confirming significant enhancement in the charge transfer efficiency in the binary and ternary nanocomposites compared to the pristine g-C3N4 sample. Hence, direct and indirect heterostructures formation promoted e-h separation and charge transfer efficiency. The lowest value of the Rct for 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite indicated that N-DCDs impregnation could highly affect the transfer and separation efficiency of the photogenerated e-h pairs. So, these results proved that N-DCDs could act as an efficient electron mediator, increasing contact conductivity which is favorable during photocatalytic reactions.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized samples under visible light radiation was evaluated using a 2 mg/L MB solution and the obtained results are depicted in Fig. 8. The MB removal efficiency in the presence of different samples was reported based on the heterogenous photocatalysis definition considering adsorption as one of the steps [61]. According to the previous reports [62,63], the dye concentration before (control sample) and after dark could be used as the initial concentration (C0), which the former employed in the present study. According to the blank experiment C/C0 vs. time plots in Fig. 8, MB was hardly degraded without photocatalyst under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8(a), under visible light irradiation, the g-C3N4 nanosheets exhibited only 59% efficiency toward MB photodegradation, which is predominantly ascribed to the quantum size effect in the nanosheets [64]. As discussed before, the band gap energy of the nanosheets was 2.97 eV due to the reduction in the number of layers by ammonium chloride exfoliation. This phenomenon could hinder the visible light utilization of the nanosheets and hence lower photocatalytic activity under visible light. However, the photodegradation efficiencies reached 98%, 100%, 97%, and 81% as 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1 wt% N-DCDs were impregnated on the g-C3N4 nanosheets. These values suggested that 0.25 wt% N-DCD was the optimized impregnation content. This observed trend, i.e., increasing and then decreasing efficiency with increasing N-DCD content, is in accordance with the literature [65,66] and could be justified through the following reasons: i) according to the UV–vis DRS results (Fig. 6(a) and (b)), all the N-DCD impregnated samples exhibited improved absorbance spectra in the visible light region and consequently reduced band gap energies. ii) PL results (Fig. 6(e)) demonstrated that N-DCD impregnation led to a significant PL intensity reduction indicating superior charge separation efficiency in N-DCD impregnated samples. iii) excessive N-DCD amounts acted as recombination centers and decreased the separation efficiency. iv) excessive N-DCD amounts competed for photon absorption (without photocatalytic activity) with g-C3N4 nanosheets, which is called the photo-shielding effect, and so a reduction in photocatalytic activity might be expected [67]. For further evaluation of the photodegradation process, a pseudo-first-order equation for the kinetic model was selected, and the activity data were fitted according to the following equation [68]:
(2) Ln (C/C0)=-kt
Where k (min−1) is the pseudo-first-order model rate constant. The results of the fitting data are shown in Fig. S4 (a), in which, accordingly, g-C3N4 and all the N-DCD impregnated samples followed the pseudo-first-order kinetic model due to the nearly 1 R2 values. Moreover, the calculated rate constants from the pseudo-first-order model are shown in Fig. 8(b). The k values for g-C3N4, 0.1 C-C3N4, 0.25 C-C3N4, 0.5 C-C3N4, and 1 C-C3N4 were 0.001, 0.018, 0.022, 0.017, and 0.008 min−1, respectively. The optimized N-DCD content on g-C3N4, i.e., 0.25 C-C3N4 sample, led to the 22-fold increase in the rate constant.

The effect of the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes on the photocatalytic activity of 0.25 C-C3N4 and g-C3N4 is demonstrated in Fig. 8(c). As illustrated, the ultra-thin nanoflakes as a visible-light responsive photocatalyst exhibited 73% efficiency in MB removal under visible light irradiation. As pointed out, the photodegradation efficiency of MB by 0.25 C-C3N4 was 100% after 180 min irradiation of visible light. In-situ growth of 2 wt% SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes on the surface of the 0.25 C-C3N4 decreased the time for complete mineralization to 120 min. Moreover, the MB photodegradation after 180 min visible light irradiation was 99%, 100%, and 99% when the in-situ growth content reached 1, 4, 8 wt% on the surface of 0.25 C-C3N4. Based on these activity results, 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 was the optimized nanocomposite toward photodegradation of MB (100%, 120 min, visible light radiation). To enlighten the role of the impregnated N-DCD, the C/C0 vs. time plot of the g-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite was also brought in Fig. 8(c). The photodegradation efficiency of MB in the presence of g-C3N4/2SnS2 was 96% after 180 min visible light irradiation, indicating that 0.25 wt% N-DCD remarkably impacted the photocatalytic properties. Furthermore, the resultant pseudo-first-order linear fitting of various samples is brought in Fig. S4 (b), implying good agreement with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model due to the high R2 value. As shown in Fig. 8(d), the calculated k value for 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite was 0.032 min−1 which was the most among the other nanocomposites, almost twice compared with the g-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. Besides, the k value for 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 was approximately 32 and 8 times higher than its constituent, g-C3N4, and SnS2, respectively. The synergistic effect between g-C3N4, SnS2, and N-DCD could explain the observed improvement. First, as confirmed by the UV–vis DRS results, in-situ growth of the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes further caused a red shift in the absorbance spectra of 0.25 C-C3N4 and reduced the band gap energy by 0.04 eV in the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. Second, heterojunction formation between 0.25 C-C3N4 and SnS2 (HRTEM image in Fig. 4(h)) resulted in significant charge separation, which was proved by a considerable reduction in PL of 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. Third, more importantly, remarkable up-conversion of the synthesized N-DCD could enhance the visible light utilization of SnS2 in the nanocomposite.

A radical scavenging test was conducted using p-BQ, EDTA, and IPA as scavenging agents of . O2 - radicals, h+, and . OH radicals to provide insight into the photocatalytic mechanism of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. As mentioned, 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite exhibited a complete degradation of MB under visible light radiation after 120 min denoting the no scavenger sample in Fig. 9(a). However, the efficiency of photodegradation decreased to 89, 71, and 50 in the presence of EDTA, IPA, and p-BQ, respectively (Fig. 9(b)). The significant reduction in photodegradation efficiency after p-BQ addition pointed out that . O2 - radicals were the major active species during photodegradation processes. Moreover, the obtained photodegradation efficiency after IPA and EDTA addition suggested that holes have participated in the formation of . OH radicals rather than direct oxidization of MB. Accordingly, the order of the active species throughout the photocatalytic reactions is superoxide >hydroxyl radicals >holes. Mott-Schottky analysis was conducted to gain further insight into the type of semiconductor, charge concentrations, flat band potentials, and band positions of the semiconductors. The obtained capacitance (1/C2) vs. potential curves of g-C3N4, SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 photocatalysts are depicted in Fig. 9(c-e). As illustrated, the positive slope of the Mott-Schottky curve in the linear region implied that the samples were n-type semiconductors and electrons were the primary charge carriers [69]. Additionally, the calculated charge carrier concentrations of g-C3N4, SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite were 3.34 × 1020, 3.95 × 1020, and 7.20 × 1020 1/cm3, respectively. This increase in charge carrier concentrations could conclude a considerable decrease in electrical resistance of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite, which strongly agrees with the PL results and efficient charge separation in the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 sample. The flat band potentials of the photocatalysts were calculated by extrapolating the linear region of the Mott-Schottky curve and finding the obtained x-intercept [70]. The calculated flat band potentials of g-C3N4, SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 concerning the calomel reference electrode were − 1.25, − 0.26, and − 1.01 eV, respectively. These values could be converted concerning the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) as the reference [71,72], which equals − 1.01, − 0.02, and − 0.77 eV for g-C3N4, SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2, respectively. The Fermi level of n-type semiconductors could be equivalent to the flat band potential when the disappearance of band bending occurs [73]. Besides, conduction band potential is another essential value estimated from flat band potential. The bottom of the conduction band for n-type semiconductors is more negative than the corresponding flat band potentials by approximately − 0.1 V. Hence, the conduction band potential of g-C3N4 and SnS2 are estimated to be − 1.11 and − 0.12 eV. The valence band potential could also be calculated by employing the estimated conduction band and band gap energies from UV–vis DRS results. Accordingly, the valence band potential of g-C3N4 and SnS2 were 1.86 and 2.08 eV. So, based on these calculations, the observed results after the radical scavenging test could be clarified. The conduction and valence band potential of the g-C3N4 nanosheets were − 1.11 and 1.86 eV (vs. NHE)). The conduction band potential is more negative than the reduction potential required for superoxide production (−0.33 eV vs. NHE), and the valence band is less positive than the required oxidation potential for hydroxyl radical production (1.99 eV vs. NHE). These data demonstrate that g-C3N4 nanosheets meet the thermodynamics requirement for superoxide production but not hydroxyl radicals, which agrees with the literature [74,75]. The valence and conduction band of the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes were estimated to be 2.08 and 0.12 eV. These values suggest that hydroxyl radicals could be produced in the presence of ultra-thin nanoflakes but not superoxide radicals [76,77]. These results have been taken into account for understanding the charge transfer mechanism throughout photocatalytic processes.

Based on the calculated band structures of g-C3N4 nanosheets and SnS2 nanoflakes by Mott-Schottky analysis, g-C3N4 nanosheets possessed conduction, valence bands, and Fermi level of − 1.11, 1.86, and − 1.01 eV (NHE), respectively. SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes had conduction, valence bands, and Fermi level of − 0.12, 2.08, and − 0.02 eV (NHE), respectively. Hence, the g-C3N4 Fermi level is more negative than the Fermi level of the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes. As confirmed by the FESEM and HRTEM images of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite, in-situ growth of the ultra-thin nanoflakes on the g-C3N4 nanosheets led to the heterojunction formation with intimate contact. After contact, i.e., heterojunction formation, free electrons transfer from g-C3N4 nanosheets with more negative Fermi level (lower work function) to the SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes with less negative Fermi level (higher work function). This phenomenon gives rise to built-in electric field formation from g-C3N4 nanosheets to SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes until equilibrium, which causes upward and downward band bending in g-C3N4 and SnS2 semiconductors [78,79]. Under these circumstances, e-h pairs are created in the valence and conduction band of g-C3N4 and SnS2 under visible light irradiation (Eq. (3)). As demonstrated by Eq. (4), Electrons in the conduction band of the SnS2 must migrate to the valence band of g-C3N4 due to the driving force of the built-in electric field ( Fig. 10). Hence, electrons and holes with less potential could recombine, and simultaneous charge separation and preserving the reduction-oxidation ability of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite could be reached. More significantly, the role of the impregnated N-DCD in Z-scheme charge transfer in the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite is in this way; firstly, the impregnated N-DCDs could work as an electron mediator between the electron in the conduction band of SnS2 and holes in the valence band of g-C3N4 (Eq. (4)). The excellent electronic conductivity of the impregnated N-DCDs is the primary reason for this phenomenon [80,81]. Secondly, nitrogen doping facilitates electron transfer from N-DCDs to the environment, which could readily produce superoxide radicals from the electrons in the g-C3N4 nanosheets conduction band (Eqs. (5)–(7)) [82]. Thirdly, the synergistic effect of the low-content SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes with the impregnated N-DCDs through which NIR light could be converted to visible light. This phenomenon could enhance the production of hydroxyl radicals by SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes (Eqs. (8) and (9)). Hence, enhancing visible light utilization, efficient charge carrier separation and transfer, and electron-accepting and donating properties of the impregnated N-DCDs have participated in photocatalytic performance improvement of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. The above discussion could be summarized through the following equations:
(3) 0·25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 + hν → 0·25 C-C3N4 (eCB, hVB) + SnS2 (eCB, hVB)
(4) N-DCD (eCB) + N-DCD (hVB) → recombination
(5) 0·25 C-C3N4 (eCB) + O2 → ·O2 -
(6) N-DCD (eCB) + O2 → ·O2 -
(7) MB + ·O2 - → intermediate products → degraded products
(8) SnS2 (hVB) + - OH → ·OH
(9) MB + ·OH → intermediate products → degraded products

The proposed Z-scheme mechanism for charge transfer throughout the photocatalytic reactions is in strong agreement with the obtained optical properties, the Mott-Schottky analysis, the radical scavenging test, and improved photocatalytic activity under visible light radiation of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite.

A recycling experiment with three cycles of photocatalytic tests was carried out to study the photostability properties of the nanocomposite with maximum activity under visible light irradiation, i.e., 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2. The obtained C/C0 vs. time plot and characterization results of the nanocomposite after the third cycle is brought in Fig. 11. As illustrated in Fig. 11(a), the efficiency of MB photodegradation of the nanocomposite after the recycling experiment for 120 min dropped by only 1%, suggesting the remarkable photostability of the synthesized nanocomposite under visible light irradiation. This negligible decline in the photodegradation efficiency might result from the slight surface inactivation and mass loss during the recycling experiment [83]. Fig. 11(b) shows the as-synthesized and used state (after the third cycle) XRD pattern of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. Comparing these two patterns provide evidence that the synthesized 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite benefits from high structural photostability under visible light irradiation. The FESEM image of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite after the third cycle with the corresponding EDS spectra from the red arrow and C, N, Sn, and S elemental images of the red-marked area are exhibited in Fig. 11(c) and (d). As depicted in Fig. 11(c), the nanosheet-based structure of the nanocomposite was maintained after three cycles of photodegradation experiments. Additionally, the high magnification FESEM image in the inset and the corresponding EDS point indicated by the red arrow in the inset prove the presence of the ultra-thin SnS2 nanoflakes on the surface of the nanosheets. The quantitative results estimate 0.56 and 1.25 at. percent Sn and S in the nanocomposite, which is close enough to the as-synthesized condition (Fig. 2(f)) and stoichiometric SnS2 values. The distribution of the constituent elements, i.e., C, N, Sn, and S atoms in the red-marked area demonstrates the uniform distribution of the elements as before the recycling experiment. Therefore, the synthesized 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite could be a reusable candidate for photocatalytic applications under visible light radiation.

A simple particulate photocatalytic method was conducted to assess the visible light H2 production performance of g-C3N4 nanosheets, SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes, g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposites. Fig. 12 exhibits the obtained results from the photocatalytic water splitting experiments under visible light radiation. As illustrated in Fig. 12(a), the produced H2 in the presence of g-C3N4, SnS2, and g-C3N4/2SnS2 photocatalysts were 317.2, 169.1, and 1021.1 μmol/g, respectively. These data indicated that improved charge separation through heterojunction formation between g-C3N4 and SnS2 could significantly affect the H2 production capability. The H2 production efficiency of the g-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite was further improved by impregnating 0.25 wt% N-DCD and reached 5050.3 μmol/g After 180 min visible light irradiation. The obtained H2 production value was approximately 5, 16, and 30 times higher than g-C3N4/2SnS2, g-C3N4, and SnS2, respectively. A zero-order equation for the kinetic model (Eq. (10)) was utilized to compare the rate constants of the H2 production reaction in the presence of different samples [84]:
(10) dCH2/dt = kH2

In this equation, CH2 is the produced H2 (μmol/g), t (h) is time, and kH2 (μmol/g.h) is the rate constant for the H2 production reaction. These data were fitted to Eq. (10) with high R2, and the fitting results are shown in Fig. S5. The calculated kH2 value for g-C3N4, SnS2, and g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 were 96.2, 55.2, 358.2, and 1622.2 μmol/g.h, respectively. So, these results confirmed that heterojunction formation and N-DCDs impregnation could significantly alter the properties of g-C3N4 toward H2 production.

A four-cycle recycling test was conducted to assess the durability of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite toward H2 production under visible light radiation. As shown in Fig. 12(c), the H2 production values after the second, third, and fourth cycles were 4909.1, 4840.9, and 4636.4 μmol/g, respectively. Moreover, the H2 production data after each cycle were fitted to Eq. (10) to calculate the kH2 value. Fitting results and the calculated kH2 values of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite after each cycle were brought in Fig. S6. These results indicated that the H2 production efficiency in the presence of 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 sample dropped by approximately 2%, 4%, and 9% after the second, third, and fourth cycles. Based on these observations, the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 sample possesses excellent durability under visible light irradiation, which introduces this nanocomposite as a highly effective candidate in the solar energy conversion field.

The LSV method was employed to verify the obtained results from the particulate photocatalytic test. As demonstrated in Fig. 12(d), the cathodic current within the range of − 1.7–0 V exhibited a significant difference in g-C3N4, SnS2, g-C3N4/2SnS2, and 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 samples. Both g-C3N4 and SnS2 samples showed negligible cathodic current, which means a restricted photoelectrochemical activity of these samples for H2 production within the recorded potential range. These low activities could be attributed to the high onset potentials of − 1.54 and − 1.33 V vs. Calomel (−1.30 and −1.09 V vs. NHE) for g-C3N4 and SnS2, respectively. However, considerable enhancement in cathodic current was observed in g-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. The onset potential for H2 production reached − 1.02 V vs. Calomel (−0.78 V vs. NHE) in the presence of this binary nanocomposite due to the efficient charge separation [85]. Because of the impregnated N-DCDs, the lowest onset potential value among the samples, − 0.47 V vs. Calomel (−0.23 V vs. NHE), belonged to the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite. This reduction in onset potential and the observed H2 production increase in the presence of 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 could be assigned to the role of N-DCD as a co-catalyst. According to the previous reports [86,87], without a proper co-catalyst with intimate contact, the high activation energy of the surface reaction, i.e., 2 H+ + 2e → H2, restricts further improvement in H2 production. Herein, the synthesis procedure for impregnating N-DCDs on the g-C3N4 nanosheets, the thermal-impregnation method, provided the intimate contact of N-DCDs co-catalysts. Hence, in addition to the roles of N-DCDs mentioned in the photocatalytic degradation section (Fig. 10), the impregnated N-DCDs in the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite could reduce the overpotential for initiating the H2 production reactions, leading to a high current density without employing a noble metal co-catalyst [88].

The photocatalytic H2 production performance of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 sample compared with the reported data in the literature. As shown in Table 1, various noble metal-free co-catalysts have been reported to improve H2 production efficiency as alternatives to noble metal co-catalyst, transition metal hydroxides, sulfides, or phosphides have been employed as the efficient co-catalyst. Comparing the data in Table 1 sheds light on the fact that carbon nanostructured, like N-DCDs, could improve the H2 production efficiency of g-C3N4-based nanocomposite under visible light irradiation more effectively.

In summary, N-DCDs was introduced as an efficient alternative to noble metal electron mediator and co-catalyst. To demonstrate this, g-C3N4 nanosheets were modified with low contents of N-DCDs and SnS2 ultra-thin nanoflakes, resulting in a novel ternary nanocomposite that features a noble metal-free electron mediator and co-catalyst. The photocatalytic performance of the samples was systematically evaluated via photocatalytic water splitting and degradation of MB under visible light radiation. Among different samples, 0.25 wt% N-DCDs and 2 wt% SnS2 was the optimized content with the highest photocatalytic water splitting (5050.3 μmol/g) and MB degradation (100%) efficiency. The improved photocatalytic performance of this nanocomposite was mainly due to the optical properties improvement, enhanced charge separation, and synergistic effect between the up-conversion of N-DCDs and SnS2. Additionally, impregnated N-DCDs acted as a noble metal-free electron mediator and co-catalyst for boosting photodegradation and H2 production owing to the decrease in the charge transfer resistance and onset potential. The estimated band positions of the semiconductors by Mott-Schottky plots and the radical scavenging results proposed a Z-scheme charge transfer through which superoxide and hydroxyl radicals were the active species throughout photodegradation processes. Finally, the optimized nanocomposite also exhibited outstanding performance during the recycling experiment. Only a 1% reduction in the photodegradation efficiency of MB after the third cycle and a 9% reduction in H2 production after the fourth cycle suggested high photostability of the 0.25 C-C3N4/2SnS2 nanocomposite.
Amir Saadati: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Validation, Writing − original draft preparation. Saeed Sheibani: Visualization, Supervision, Writing − review & editing.
